coredata.dc:title: Sustainable transformation of bio-waste into bio-compatible carbon dots for highly specific and sensitive detection of monosodium glutamate in food products
coredata.dc:creator: Kumari, Manisha
Banger, Kirandeep
Ram Chaudhary, Ganga
Chaudhary, Savita
Umar, Ahmad
Akbar, Sheikh
Baskoutas, Sotirios
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple and convenient sensory platform based on the fluorescence quenching and paper-based probe has been developed using carbon dots (CQDs). The CQDs prepared from waste rice straw (RS@CQDs) as a precursor source. Significantly, low value of detection (1.33 nM) and a wide linear response (5 nM to 80 μM), as well as good stability and repeatability endowed them to be highly sensitive and efficient probe for the quantitative analysis of monosodium glutamate (MSG) ions. Interferences from various biological compounds were also investigated for the developed sensor. Further, the RS@CQDs-based sensor efficiently detected MSG ions in real food samples. The portable and highly sensory podium for the visual detection of MSG in aqueous samples with LOD as low as 1 nm has also been prepared.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Waste rice straw
Monosodium glutamate
Paper-based sensor
Fluorescence quenching
Real food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of monosodium glutamate using RS@CQDs
Detection of MSG in real food samples
Development of paper-based sensor for rapid detection of MSG via using RS@CQDs
Detection of MSG in real food samples
Paper-based sensing probe for rapid detection of MSG via using RS@CQDs

Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Manisha Kumari is thankful to Haryana State Council for Science and Technology, Panchkula for financial support under HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476. Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476], DST Chandigarh [S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(19-20)/Sanc/12/2019/1878-1885], and [S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(22-23)/Sanc/09/2022/907-915] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Ganga Ram is thankful to DST, SERB/F/8171/2015-16 and UGC (F. No. 194-2/2016 IC) for providing financial support. The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University, Najran, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for funding under the Research Group funding program grant no. NU/RG/SRC/12/49.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images with SAED graph (inset), (B) FESEM, (C) XRD spectrum, Emission spectrum of RS@CQDs (D) at different excitation wavelengths, (E) as a function of concentration and (F) as a function of pH of reaction medium for RS@CQDs.

Photo-stability analysis of RS@CQDs as a function of (A) different physiological conditions, (B) optical images of the solution containing developed Rs@CQDs under UV irradiation (360 nm), (C) long-term photostability, (D) influence of UV-exposure time on the emission profile, (E) effect of ionic strength, (F) variation in the fluorescence intensity at different temperature, (G) XPS graph shown the existence of carbon and oxygen peaks and (Gi-Gii) XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s spectrum of RS@CQDs.
(A) TGA graph, (B) probable mechanistic behaviour of TGA profile and (C) DSC curves of CQDs derived from waste rice-straw.

PL emission spectrum (A) in the presence of various chosen biological substances and metal ions, (B) with different concentrations of MSG, (C) influence of potential interfering biomolecules, (D) Stern-Volmer, (E) B-H plot and (F) obtained values of detection limit, binding constant, quantitation limit, quenching factor and fluorescence recovery RS@CQDs.

(A) Percentage recovery studies for MSG in vegetable soup and tomato sauce by RS@CQDs. (B) Digital images showing the paper-based sensing potential of RS@CQDs in presence of various biomolecules and metal ions along with the variations of different concentrations of MSG from 0 μm to 700 μm in the presence of RS@CQDs coated paper probe.
Digital photographs of PVA/RS@CQDs fluorescent films under the illumination of (A) visible light and (B) UV light (360 nm).
The illustration of the possible quenching mechanism of RS@CQDs by MSG.
Comparison between the forecasting outcomes with the reported papers and currently produced sensor.

row.entry: glutamate oxidase and peroxidase based sensor

enzymatic sensor based on
oxygen electrode

head.ce:title: Sustainable transformation of bio-waste into bio-compatible carbon dots for highly specific and sensitive detection of monosodium glutamate in food products

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science, University of Patras, 26500 Patras, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple and convenient sensory platform based on the fluorescence quenching and paper-based probe has been developed using carbon dots (CQDs). The CQDs prepared from waste rice straw (RS@CQDs) as a precursor source. Significantly, low value of detection (1.33 nM) and a wide linear response (5 nM to 80 μM), as well as good stability and repeatability endowed them to be highly sensitive and efficient probe for the quantitative analysis of monosodium glutamate (MSG) ions. Interferences from various biological compounds were also investigated for the developed sensor. Further, the RS@CQDs-based sensor efficiently detected MSG ions in real food samples. The portable and highly sensory podium for the visual detection of MSG in aqueous samples with LOD as low as 1 nm has also been prepared.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The sodium salt of glutamate (MSG) is one of the primary non-essential amino acids and has been extensively utilized to boost sensory essence in food products.[1] The vital role of MSG in pharmaceuticals and various physiological activities like the biosynthesis of amino acid, metabolic activities and gut-brain axis activation is well documented in the literature.[2] MSG serves as one of the important excitatory neurotransmitters in the central nervous system and is responsible for various behavioural activities such as visual-task learning, cognition, aggressiveness, memory and cellular metabolism in living beings.[3] Additionally, MSG is also referred to as the “workhouse of the brain”. MSG has the ability to reinforce synaptic connections and has the ability to establish different pathways across the brain, and supports in analyzing the brain’s energy balance dynamics in human beings.[4] However, excessive MSG consumption in food products have the tendency to elevate the glutamate levels in the cerebrospinal fluids and has the ability to trigger neurological symptoms including epilepsy, stroke, Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease, as well as allergic reactions including headache, digestive problems, chest pain and numbness.[5] Hence, glutamate sensing with accuracy in different food products is necessary for getting information about the quality of food products and treating primary neurological illnesses in patients.[6].

Upto now, capillary electrophoresis, mass spectroscopic, and chromatographic and potentiometric methods have been used for estimating the presence of glutamate. However, the extensive procedure with costly equipment and the requirement of trained people for getting real-time monitoring information has restricted their usage in large-scale sensing of MSG in food products.[7] In addition, the usage of sensitive and selective biosensors using nanomaterials is highly useful for the rapid and selective detection of glutamate.[8] Upto now, different types of nanomaterials including metal oxides and semiconducting materials have been used for the detection of glutamate. For instance, tricobalt tetraoxide nanoparticles (Co3O4) have been utilized by Dalkıran et al. for the detection of glutamate by using electrochemical methods.[9] However; the developed sensor has lost its 45% initial activity toward glutamate in three weeks. Au@MoS2/Chitosan modified glassy carbon electrode has displayed 0.03 and 0.1 μM as the limit of detection and limit of quantification values for MSG via electrochemical detection of MSG.[10] Alnokkari et al. have used the application of 4-aminoantipyrine and phenol as the sensing probe for the estimation of MSG in food samples with sensitivity up to 25 mg and has the detection limit of 0.5 mg.[11] However, the utilization of carbon dots (CQDs) derived from waste biomass for the detection of MSG is less explored.[12] Therefore, the current work aims at the application of carbon dots (CQDs) derived from waste agricultural biomass for the quantification of MSG.

Every year, around 7.6 billion metric tons of generated agricultural waste including sugarcane bagasse, corn stover, wheat stem and rice residues is wasted due to the non-availability of proper and cost-effective waste management systems. However, rice being a staple food item and highly consumed worldwide, it has been found that around 800–1000 million tonnes per year of rice straw have been produced at a global scale with 600–800 million tonnes produced in Asia alone.[13] Rice-straw is a poor feed compared to other cereal straws and when burned directly to produce steam, it has caused severe furnace fouling and ash output is considered one of the primary sources of pollution in northern India.[14] In this regard, the simple and appropriate technologies for recycling rice-straw into useful and important products are highly required for managing environmental challenges.[15] Being a rich source of carbon, these waste rice straws have been utilized for the synthesis of CQDs with high oxygen and carbon-containing functional groups over their exterior surface. Out of diverse range of promising nanomaterials, CQDs have achieved potential significance due to their high biocompatibility, unique optical and fluorescence properties with simple and economical synthesis.[16–20] The presence of different types of surface function groups has further helped in enhancing the electrostatic interaction with MSG and supported the charge transfer properties.[21] The analysis is carried out using fluorescence spectroscopy as a function of different analytes and concentrations of MSG in the analyte solution. Additionally, CQDs have further been used for the formation of fluorescent hybrid polymer films (PVA/CQDs). The overall performance of the developed sensor is further verified by comparing the selectivity and sensitivity in different real food samples to enhance the practical utilization of the developed sensor. A recyclable and sensitive paper-based sensor has also been developed using CQDs for the quantitative detection of MSG. This novel paper-based sensor is based on the advanced fluorescent properties of CQDs which has produced changes in the color of the paper strips in the presence of MSG. The simplicity of the developed sensor has made it an appropriate substitute for the detection of MSG. To our knowledge, the application of CQDs derived from waste rice straw has not yet been explored for the fabrication of paper-based sensory strips for the detection of MSG. The high-response rate towards MSG detection method using CQDs has provided a rapid and effective solution for quality control in food, agriculture and environmental monitoring.

Waste rice-straw residues as a carbon source were obtained from the village Rajpura, District Jind, Haryana, India. Double distilled water was used throughout all the experiment. All the used metal ions and biomolecules such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, NH4+, K+, Na2+, Fe2+, aspartic acid, ascorbic acid, glycine, citric acid, cysteine, serine and MSG were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India with purity > 98%. The polyvinyl alcohol, sodium chloride (NaCl), tris-HCl, sodium hydroxide salt (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were procured from fisher Scientific, India with 99% purity. Whatman filter paper was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. The real food samples which include vegetable soup and tomato sauce were acquired from a local market of sector-14, Chandigarh, India.

The current study aimed to provide an efficient recycling means to convert waste biomass of rice-straw as a carbon source to highly fluorescent CQDs via a simple one-step synthesis method. Initially, the rice-straw residues were collected and washed using water and ethanol solution followed by grounding to fine powder. The obtained fine powder of rice straw was subjected to thermal calcination at 250 °C for 4 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. The calcined sample was allowed to cool down to room temperature and ground to generate uniform and fine particles of carbon. Subsequently, 4 gm of obtained powder was dissolved in distilled water and set for constant stirring at 300 rpm for 24 h to get the homogenous carbonized solution. The as-formed solution was then allowed to ultracentrifuge at 6500 rpm for 11 min to form a suspension containing CQDs. Afterward, the filtered dispersion was placed in a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) 1000 Da and set up to allow dialysis for 24 h. The respective suspension was allowed to heat at 100 °C to get RS@CQDs (CQDs obtained from waste rice-straws and stored in dry conditions for further characterization and application testing.

The structural information about RS@CQDs was obtained by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer RX1). The powder X-ray diffraction (Panalytical X’Pert Pro X-ray diffractometer) profile was recorded between 5 and 90○ at a scan speed of 2°min−1. The morphological studies were observed using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) (Hitachi H7500, 100 kV). Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM 7500F) was utilized to assess the structural variations produced in RS@CQDs. The digital Lab India pH meter was applied to conduct pH experiments. The fluorescence emission profiles were obtained using Perkin Elmer LS 55 instrument in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The thermal analysis of RS@CQDs was performed on a Mettler Toledo Q20 differential scanning calorimetric (DSC) device. The hydrodynamic diameter was estimated using a dynamic light scattering device (MALVERN ZEN 1690). The UV absorption studies were carried out on Jasco V-750 UV–visible spectrophotometer in the working range of 200–800 nm. The thermal calcination was carried out in an AICIL muffle furnace. The TGA-DTA data was collected on SDT Q-600 instrument. The ultra-centrifugation was carried out on a Remi-ultracentrifuge machine. XPS analysis was done on Physical Electronics, Model PHI 500 Versa Probe III instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of monosodium glutamate using RS@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selective detection of MSG was done using RS@CQDs by employing standard fluorescence detection technique. For the analysis, the fluorescence emission studies of RS@CQDs were carried out in the presence of variety of metal ions and biomolecules, including Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Cu2+, Na2+, Fe2+, NH4 +, ascorbic acid, aspartic acid, citric acid, glycine, serine, cysteine and MSG. For the measurement, the fluorescence emission of an incubated equi-volume solution of each specified analyte molecule with RS@CQDs in aqueous media was measured in the range of 200–800 nm. Additionally, the detection sensitivity of developed sensor was analyzed by adding a fixed concentration of RS@CQDs in the presence of various selected analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MSG in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Two different food samples, i.e., vegetable soup and tomato sauce having known amounts of MSG (40 and 80 mg, respectively) were tested in order to investigate the future potential applications of developed biosensor in the food industry. Adequate pre-treatment procedures were applied for both types of samples. In particular, vegetable soup and tomato sauce were diluted with phosphate buffer solution (pH − 9.0) followed by the centrifugation of the respective solutions at 7500 rpm for 15 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected and filtered using a 0.42 m Millipore filter and diluted up to 10 fold with deionized water before detection. The chosen samples were spiked with different concentrations of MSG (i.e., 10 μm, 20 μm, 30 μm, 40 μm and 50 μm) for the relevant fluorescence-based detection in the presence of RS@CQDs. The corresponding emission spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The following equation was used to calculate the recovery values:
(1)
Re cov e r
( % )
=
C I
-
C R
C x
Here, CR stands for the concentration of MSG in the chosen food samples prior to the addition of the external standard solution of MSG and Cx stands for the quantity of MSG mixed in real food samples. CI denotes the concentration of MSG in real food samples after dilution with the standard solution of MSG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of paper-based sensor for rapid detection of MSG via using RS@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A paper-based sensor has provided a mobile and on-site detection of MSG. For the preparation, Whatman filter paper with a diameter of 4 cm was cut into circular pieces. Each piece of paper was submerged in 1 ml of the synthesized RS@CQDs solution and the filter paper was removed after 30 s. The respective papers were then allowed to naturally dry. Afterward, a few drops of MSG with different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 and 700 μm) were added to RS@CQDs modified filter paper. After drying, the filter paper sensor was exposed to Ultraviolet radiation with λ = 360 nm. All the experimentations were measured twice to verify the accuracy of the developed sensor.

For the experimentation, around 3 g of PVA was thoroughly mixed with deionized water (20 ml) under stirrings conditions. For preparing proper dispersion, the mixture was subsequently heated to 80 °C for 1 h. Different concentrations of RS@CQDs containing solution (2 ml, 2.5 ml, 3 ml, 3.5 ml, 4 ml, 4.5 ml and 5 ml) were mixed with aqueous solution of PVA. The formed solutions were allowed to agitate for additional 15 min. Afterward, the resultant solution was poured into a fresh Petri dish and dried for 12 h at 44 °C in an oven for getting fluorescence films of RS@CQDs.

The developed methodology to convert waste rice husk to advanced CQDs is an example of the effective utilization ofbiomass waste for the development of efficient biosensor. Under optimal synthesis conditions, the size and morphology of prepared CQDs were checked through HRTEM and XRD analysis. The HRTEM images have displayed the existence of spherical-shaped CQDs with good dispersibility and uniform morphology (Fig. 1 A). The average diameter of RS@CQDs was around 5–10 nm. The lattice fringe spacing was determined to be 0.21 nm which was assigned to the (100) plane of the carbon. The results obtained from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern have further supported the crystalline nature of synthesized CQDs (Figure inset 1A). The resultant FESEM image has displayed the existence of relatively irregular-shaped RS@CQDs with large lumps due to agglomeration and growth of particles in solid state (Fig. 1B). However, the equivalent hydrodynamic radius of RS@CQDs was determined to be 21 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) value of 0.3 ( Figure S1
supplementary data ). The XRD profile has supported the crystalline nature of formed particles (Fig. 1C). The peaks obtained for RS@CQDs, along with their respective crystal planes have been presented in Table S1 . The measured crystalline size was found to be 34.8 nm which confirms the high crystalline nature of RS@CQDs.

The emission spectra of the RS@CQDs were also investigated as a function of excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1D). The emission spectra of RS@CQDs were found to be red-shifted from 420 nm to 518 nm with increasing excitation wavelengths from 290 to 470 nm. However, the emission intensity was found to be maximum at 425 nm via exciting the sample with λ = 330 nm. The respective bathochromic transition in the emission spectrum was mainly explained due to the existence of different emissive trap site distributions, recombination in the radiation of excitons and quantum effect in RS@CQDs.[22] The photogenerated electrons and holes trapped at various surface sites of the produced CQDs and connected to their radiative recombination were primarily responsible for these variations in emission intensity. It is obvious that the majority of the excited electrons were captured by various energy levels and reverted to ground states which in return produce their characteristic fluorescence emission. Moreover, PL characteristics were also influenced by the functional groups existing on the surface of RS@CQDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission properties of RS@CQDs were found to be dependent on concentration (Fig. 1E). The spectra displayed the linear increment in the emission intensity of RS@CQDs with the increase in concentration and further decreased at a very high concentration (0.4–2.0 mg/mL) of CQDs. This observation might be the result of the growing interaction between formed particles. Moreover, the presence of polar functional groups further enhanced the agglomeration rate at very high concentrations of RS@CQDs.

The PL intensity of RS@CQDs has also displayed an increment as the pH of the reaction medium varied from 1 to 5 (Fig. 1F). Afterward, the intensity remained relatively constant between pH ranges from 7 to 11 (Fig. 1F). However, the enhancement in the pH value of reaction medium has led to decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity of RS@CQDs Fig. 1F). Figure S2 displayed the UV–visible absorption spectra of RS@CQDs. On interpreting the spectrum, a significant absorption peak at 270 nm and a shoulder peak at 370 nm were observed for RS@CQDs. The conjugated C = C groups from the carbon core performed a blue-shifted transition, which caused a peak to develop at 270 nm. Meanwhile, the shoulder peak was linked to the presence of C = O groups due to the n-π* transition, which is a common characteristic of fluorescent CQDs.[23] These observed results further enhance the chemo-sensing performance of prepared CQDs from waste rice straw.

The stability analysis of RS@CQDs is highly significant to ensure their utilization in biosensing applications. For this purpose, the emission intensity of RS@CQDs at different physiological conditions such as pH = 7.4, 0.17 mM NaCl, and 12 mM Tris-HCl was employed to investigate the photostability of RS@CQDs. It has been found that the emission intensity remained unaffected in the presence of chosen conditions (Fig. 2 A). The fluorescence emission intensity was found to display no degeneration as a function of time (Fig. 2B and 2C). The fluorescence intensity remained the same for developed RS@CQDs even after 30 days. Additionally, continuous exposure to UV light for around 120 min has barely influenced the PL intensity of RS@CQDs (Fig. 2D). Using NaCl as a reference, the influencing effect of ionic strength has also been examined for the chemical reactivity and photo-stability of RS@CQDs (Fig. 2E). On interpreting the data, it has been observed that the ionic strength has produced no impact on the fluorescence intensity of the RS@CQDs on increasing the concentrations of NaCl from 0.1 to 0.5 g in the reaction medium. Additionally, the emission intensity of developed RS@CQDs has scarcely impacted under different temperature conditions ranging from 20 to 70 °C (Fig. 2F). The results have clearly demonstrated that RS@CQDs have possessed an exceptionally high range of photostability and have higher scope in the field of sensing.

Figure S3 displays the FTIR spectra of RS@CQDs. The spectrum displays the characteristic peaks at 3102 cm−1 due to the presence of O–H stretching in RS@CQDs.[24] The absorption bands at 1664 cm−1 have been attributed to the C = O and C = C stretching vibrations.[25] The presence of C-O and O-C-O bonds also gave signature peaks at 1223 cm−1. The occurrence of peak at 714 cm−1 has been assigned to the C–H bending vibration present at the outer surface of RS@CQDs.[26] As per the XPS survey scan of RS@CQDs, the prepared particles are primarily composed of carbon and oxygen, with a respective amount of C1s (71.4 atomic %) and O1s (28.54 atomic %) without displaying the other trace peaks from any other residual elements (Fig. 2G).

The deconvolution of C 1 s peak depicted the presence of three characteristic peaks with the binding energy values of 284.77 eV, 286.11 eV and 288.11 eV related to the C = C, C-O-C/C-O and C = O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2Gi).[27] The fact that carbon atoms were functionalized with oxygen confirmed the high degree of functionalization in RS@CQDs. The deconvolution of O 1 s peak was ascribed to the corresponding peaks at 531.5 eV, 532.88 eV and 533.15 eV which were attributed to the C = O, C-O and C-OH functional groups, respectively (Fig. 2Gii).[28] The obtained results from the XPS survey scan were well supplemented with the FTIR spectrum as the prepared RS@CQDs acquired with high concentrations of hydrophilic groups on their surfaces. The presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of produced RS@CQDs is responsible for their significant binding with the MSG ions. According to the FT-IR and XPS data, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups fully bonded to the surface of the produced CQDs, which can be crucial in the labelling of MSG ions. These comparison findings are very useful in explaining the fluorescence sensing abilities of developed RS@CQDs.

TGA analysis was further performed to determine the thermal stabilities of the RS@CQDs in the temperature range of 0–800 °C ( Fig. 3 A). Three significant weight losses between the temperature ranges of 110 °C to 550 °C were observed during the thermal degradation of RS@CQDs. The first weight loss obtained below the temperature range of 260 °C was due to the vaporization of moisture content present from RS@CQDs. The next weight loss in the region of 360 °C to 555 °C was attributed to the elimination of oxygen-containing functional moieties from the prepared particles. The third weight loss was due to the removal of pyro-gases and oxidative degradation of the exterior functionalities from the surface of RS@CQDs (Fig. 3B). These observations supported the remarkable thermal constancy and purity of the bio-mass waste-derived CQDs. Furthermore, under a nitrogen atmosphere, the exothermic character of RS@CQDs was revealed using DSC analysis (Fig. 3C). The respective values of glass transition temperature were found to be 82.99 °C and 310.06 °C which endorsed the remarkable thermal stability of RS@CQDs.

Furthermore, the titration-metric method has been used to explore the sensitivity of the designed bio-sensor by observing the changes in the fluorescence behavior of formed RS@CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of MSG ions (5 nM to 80 μM). On interpreting the data, it has been found that the emission intensity of RS@CQDs has shown linear decrease with increasing the concentration of MSG in reaction media (Fig. 4B). The high sensitivity and accuracy of the proposed sensor further enhanced the efficiency of the designed bio-sensory probe for turn-off fluorescence intensity in the presence of MSG ions.

Further, Stern-Volmer analysis has been used to clarify the quenching mechanism of the proposed sensor.
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
Here, F0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity in the absence of a quencher, and F represents the fluorescence intensity in the presence of a quencher. KSV is the Stern-Volmer association constant and Q is the amount of MSG ions in the solution.[32] The detection limit value was determined using the standard deviation rule given below:
(3)
LOD = 3 S D / P
Where, SD stands for the standard deviation of the blank sample and P refers to the slope of the calibration curve (Fig. 4D). Additionally, the Benesi-Hildebrand plot was utilized to detect the binding effect of the proposed bio-sensor as presented in Fig. 4E. Table 1 contains the calculated values for the detection limit, binding constant, fluorescence quenching factor and quantization constant as illustrated in Fig. 4F. The as-prepared sensor has possessed the detection limit value of around 1.33 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MSG in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed nanoprobe has further been used to examine the efficacy of the RS@CQDs-based fluorescence sensor for MSG detection in real food samples. For the analysis, different concentrations of the RS@CQDs (10 μM, 30 μM, 40 μM and 50 μM) have been added to the pre-treated food samples of vegetable soup and tomato sauce. The fluorescence emission profile has been used to evaluate the emission peak intensity via exciting the sample with λ = 330 nm. The outcomes have supported excellent accuracy and high recovery values in the range of 88–93% for RS@CQDs (Fig. 5 A). This conclusion demonstrated that prepared RS@CQDs have high potential to detect MSG ions in real-time applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based sensing probe for rapid detection of MSG via using RS@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A paper-based sensory probe for the detection of MSG was further developed to explore the practical application of RS@CQDs. The chosen filter papers were submerged in RS@CQDs solution for 30 s before being left to dry naturally. Fig. 5B depicts the RS@CQDs tethered paper-based sensor after the addition of various biological substances. The paper strips emitted cyan color when exposed to UV light and immersed in a CQDs-containing solution.

However, the corresponding cyan emission got darkened largely in the presence of MSG which further indicated the selective sensing of MSG via using the coated paper strips with RS@CQDs. Additionally, the variations of different concentration of MSG from 0 μM to 700 μM, has displayed the progressive development of darkened cyan color on paper strips and has supported the visual detection of MSG via employing RS@CQDs based sensory probe. These findings have amply demonstrated the viability of using a paper-based sensor probe for the selective detection of MSG visually.

Due to its remarkable characteristics like hydrophilicity, transparency, thermal stability, non-toxicity and film-forming activity, PVA (a synthetic polymer that is soluble in water) is well recognized for various applications in the fields of optoelectronics, packaging of foods, and membrane and plastic film fabrication. To establish the viability of the RS@CQDs as luminous polymeric films, different concentrations of RS@CQDs aqueous solution (2 ml-5 ml) were combined with PVA solution and the resulting mixture was dried at 44 °C. Figure 6 displays the digital images of prepared fluorescent polymeric films with different concentrations of RS@CQDs under visible and UV light conditions. As illustrated in Figure 6 A, the prepared films were remarkably transparent when exposed to visible light, indicating good dispersion of RS@CQDs in the PVA matrix. Further, the fabricated luminescent films emitted cyan color under UV light illumination of λ = 360 nm (Figure 6 B). The emission color of the developed RS@CQDs-based fluorescent films has been comparable to that of RS@CQDs solutions. The outcomes confirmed the potential application of RS@CQDs in packaging and optoelectronic devices.

In summary, rice straw, a non-food biomass waste served as an eco-friendly precursor for the synthesis of CQDs. The developed RS@CQDs displayed excellent aqueous solubility, photo-stability and stability in all environmental conditions. The synthesized RS@CQDs demonstrated high fluorescence signal with an emissive peak at 450 nm via exciting the sample λ = 330 nm. Further, the prepared particles displayed high potential for sensing MSG in a single step without any kind of surface modification of RS@CQDs. It was found that the presence of MSG has significantly reduced the fluorescence signal of developed RS@CQDs. The low detection limit (1.33 nM), a wide linear response (5 nM to 80 μM) and cost-effective behavior of fabricated RS@CQDs-based nanoprobes endowed them with highly sensitive and efficient monitoring probes for MSG. Interferences from various other chosen biological substances were also investigated for the produced sensor. Moreover, the synthesized RS@CQDs was utilized to form a new paper-based sensory probe for MSG by direct visualization. The detection was achieved to be rapid, low-cost and easy but found to be quite sensitive. This novel detection approach proved to be appealing and promising for MSG detection. Additionally, the developed RS@CQDs was expanded to fabricate the highly transparent and strongly fluorescent PVA/CQDs films with enhanced potential in food-packaging fluorescent material and optoelectronic devices. The present work not only established a new approach for the formation of biosensors but also opened affordable and reliable access to extremely fluorescent RS@CQDs for a variety of applications.

Manisha Kumari: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Kirandeep Banger: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ganga Ram Chaudhary: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Supervision. Savita Chaudhary: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ahmad Umar: Data curation, Visualization. Sheikh Akbar: Data curation, Validation. Sotirios Baskoutas: Methodology, Data curation, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University, Najran, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for funding under the Research Group funding program grant no. NU/RG/SRC/12/49.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2023.122825.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
